opts: Use Dataflow's exactly-once processing guarantee by enabling update mode for transforms and using BigQuery streaming inserts with insertIds
Enable deduplication in the Pub/Sub subscription with exactly-once delivery
Use BigQuery merge statements instead of inserts to handle duplicates
Add a deduplication transform in Dataflow using a Stateful DoFn
explain: Dataflow provides exactly-once processing semantics when configured correctly: use update mode for stateful transforms and use BigQuery streaming inserts with insertIds (deterministic based on message). Pub/Sub exactly-once delivery helps but isn't sufficient alone for end-to-end exactly-once (Dataflow must also participate). MERGE statements handle duplicates reactively but don't prevent them (less efficient). A stateful deduplication DoFn is possible but Dataflow's built-in guarantees are preferred. For batch pipelines, Dataflow provides exactly-once automatically; for streaming, use appropriate sinks with idempotent writes.
concept: Dataflow exactly-once: Enabled by default for batch. For streaming: use idempotent sinks (BigQuery with insertIds, Bigtable, Spanner). Combine with Pub/Sub exactly-once delivery. Dataflow checkpoints state and uses distributed snapshots for fault tolerance.

q: Your Dataflow pipeline uses a GroupByKey operation that shows a hot key warning in the UI. One key has 10 million values while others have <100. Processing is slow. What optimization should you apply?
opts: Use Combine.perKey instead of GroupByKey if possible, and add fanout to distribute hot key processing
Increase the number of workers to parallelize GroupByKey
Split the hot key into multiple sub-keys in upstream processing
Switch to session windows to distribute values over time
explain: Combine.perKey is more efficient than GroupByKey when performing aggregations - it does partial combining before shuffling, reducing data transfer. For hot keys, Combine.perKey with .withFanout() distributes hot key processing across multiple workers by adding a random shard key, combining partially, then final combining. This is the best optimization. Increasing workers helps but doesn't address the hot key directly (all values still go to one worker). Splitting keys upstream requires application logic changes. Session windows address temporal distribution, not hot keys.
concept: Dataflow hot key: One key with many values causes worker skew. Solutions: use Combine.perKey with .withFanout() to distribute hot keys, redesign keys to distribute more evenly, use approximate algorithms (Count-Min Sketch) for aggregations. Monitor via UI metrics.

q: Your streaming Dataflow pipeline processes e-commerce events. You need to count events per user in 5-minute fixed windows, but also want to update results as new late data arrives for up to 10 minutes after the window. What should you configure?
opts: Fixed windows (5 minutes) with allowed lateness (10 minutes) and accumulating mode with early and late triggers
Sliding windows (5 minutes) with 10-minute duration
Session windows with 5-minute gap and 10-minute timeout
Fixed windows (5 minutes) with global trigger every 10 minutes
explain: This requires: 1) Fixed windows (5 min) for the basic window structure, 2) Allowed lateness (10 min) to accept late data, 3) Accumulating mode (each trigger includes all data) instead of discarding mode, 4) Early triggers to emit results before window closes, and 5) Late triggers to emit updates when late data arrives. This pattern balances timely results with completeness. Sliding windows create overlapping windows (not what's asked). Session windows are for activity-based grouping. Global trigger doesn't respect event time.
concept: Dataflow triggers: Control when results are emitted. Early = before watermark (for speculative results), On-time = at watermark, Late = after watermark (for late data). Accumulating mode = each emission includes all data. Use with allowed lateness for complete-but-updated results.

q: Your Dataflow pipeline has inconsistent throughput - sometimes processing quickly, sometimes lagging with high system lag. The pipeline reads from Pub/Sub, does CPU-intensive transformations, and writes to BigQuery. What should you investigate first?
opts: Check if autoscaling is enabled and if there are worker CPU/memory bottlenecks in Dataflow metrics
Increase the number of Pub/Sub subscriptions to parallelize reads
Enable BigQuery streaming inserts instead of batch loads
Increase the Dataflow pipeline's max worker count
explain: Variable throughput suggests resource bottlenecks or autoscaling issues. Check Dataflow metrics: CPU utilization (>80% indicates CPU bottleneck), memory usage, system lag (backlog age). Ensure autoscaling is enabled so Dataflow can add workers during load. If CPU is maxed, consider optimizing transforms or using more powerful machine types. Multiple Pub/Sub subscriptions don't help - Dataflow automatically parallelizes reading from one subscription. BigQuery streaming vs batch doesn't affect upstream processing speed. Increasing max workers helps if autoscaling is hitting limits, but first verify autoscaling is working.
concept: Dataflow performance debugging: Check metrics: system lag (backlog age), CPU/memory utilization, autoscaling events. System lag = watermark behind wall clock. High CPU/memory = need more workers or optimization. Dataflow UI provides per-step metrics.

q: You need to store 10 PB of historical video files accessed once a year for compliance. Retrieval within hours is acceptable. Costs must be minimized. What Cloud Storage class should you use?
opts: Archive Storage - lowest cost for data accessed less than once a year
Coldline Storage - low cost for data accessed less than once a quarter
Nearline Storage - low cost for data accessed less than once a month
Standard Storage with lifecycle policy to delete after one year
explain: Archive Storage is the lowest-cost storage class ($0.0012/GB/month vs Coldline $0.004/GB/month), designed for data accessed less than once a year. Retrieval within hours is acceptable (minimum storage duration: 365 days). This is the exact use case Archive is designed for. Coldline ($0.004/GB/month, minimum 90 days) is for quarterly access. Nearline ($0.010/GB/month, minimum 30 days) is for monthly access. Standard ($0.020/GB/month) is most expensive. Deleting after a year violates compliance requirement to retain.
concept: Storage class selection: Standard = hot data, frequent access. Nearline = <1/month access. Coldline = <1/quarter access. Archive = <1/year access. Trade-off: lower storage cost, higher retrieval cost + minimum storage duration penalty if deleted early.

q: Your application stores user-uploaded images in Cloud Storage Standard class. Analytics show 80% of images are never accessed after 7 days, 15% are accessed occasionally (monthly), and 5% remain frequently accessed. How should you optimize costs?
opts: Create a lifecycle policy: transition to Nearline after 7 days, and to Coldline after 90 days
Immediately move all objects to Coldline Storage
Create a lifecycle policy: transition to Archive after 7 days
Keep everything in Standard and enable object versioning
explain: Lifecycle policies automate transitions based on object age. Transition to Nearline after 7 days (cheaper storage for 80% rarely accessed, plus 15% occasional access fits Nearline). For objects still in Nearline after 90 days (rarely accessed), transition to Coldline for even lower cost. The 5% frequently accessed objects will generate retrieval costs in Nearline, but the overall savings from the 80% rarely accessed justifies this. Moving everything to Coldline immediately would cause high retrieval costs for the 5% frequently accessed. Archive's 365-day minimum is too aggressive. Object versioning doesn't reduce costs.
concept: Lifecycle policy pattern: Standard → Nearline (7-30 days) → Coldline (90 days) → Archive (365 days) or Delete. Base on access patterns. Early deletion before minimum storage duration incurs penalty. Test with smaller buckets first.

q: You need to provide a temporary download link for a private object in Cloud Storage to an external user who doesn't have a Google account. The link should expire after 1 hour. What should you use?
opts: Generate a signed URL with 1-hour expiration using a service account key
Make the bucket public and share the object URL, then make it private after 1 hour
Create a Cloud Function that proxies the download and shares the function URL
Add the user's email to bucket IAM with Storage Object Viewer role, then remove after 1 hour
explain: Signed URLs provide time-limited access to specific objects without requiring authentication. Generate using gsutil signurl or code with a service account key, specifying expiration. The URL includes a signature that validates until expiration. This is the standard pattern for temporary access. Making the bucket public is insecure (anyone with the URL can access). Cloud Function proxy works but is more complex and adds latency. IAM roles require Google account and manual removal - not suitable for external users.
concept: Signed URLs: Time-limited access to private objects without authentication. Generated with service account key. Use cases: temporary downloads, uploads, CDN integration. Max 7-day expiration. Alternative: V4 signing for longer URLs with more features.

q: Your Cloud Storage bucket receives millions of small files (<10 KB) daily. You need to optimize for list operations and range reads. What should you do?
opts: Enable hierarchical namespace (HNS) on the bucket for optimized directory operations
Combine small files into larger objects before storage
Use composite objects to merge small files
Enable object versioning to improve metadata access
explain: Small files are inefficient in Cloud Storage: high request costs, slow list operations, and overhead. Combining small files into larger objects (e.g., tar archives or batched files) dramatically improves performance and reduces costs. Each object has overhead, so fewer large objects > many small objects. Hierarchical namespace (HNS) improves directory operations but doesn't solve the small file problem. Composite objects are created by composing multiple source objects, but you'd still need to upload the small objects first. Object versioning is for history retention, not performance.
concept: Cloud Storage small file problem: Many small files = high overhead, slow lists, expensive requests. Solutions: batch into larger objects, use tar/zip, aggregate before upload. Target: >1 MB objects for efficiency. If small files needed, consider Bigtable or Filestore instead.

q: Your data pipeline uses Cloud Storage for intermediate data. Files are written once, read multiple times within 24 hours, then never accessed. You need to automatically delete files after 30 days. What configuration should you use?
opts: Store in Standard class with lifecycle policy: Delete after 30 days
Store in Nearline class with lifecycle policy: Delete after 30 days
Store in Standard class with retention policy of 30 days
Store in Standard class and use Cloud Scheduler to delete old files weekly
explain: Standard class is appropriate since files are read multiple times within 24 hours (frequent access). Lifecycle policy with 'Delete' action after 30 days age automates cleanup. Nearline has minimum storage duration of 30 days and higher retrieval costs - since files are accessed multiple times, you'd pay retrieval fees multiple times. Retention policies PREVENT deletion (for compliance), not enable it. Cloud Scheduler works but is manual implementation of what lifecycle policies provide automatically.
concept: Lifecycle policy actions: Delete (remove object), SetStorageClass (transition to cheaper class). Conditions: Age, CreatedBefore, CustomTimeBefore, DaysSinceNoncurrentTime, IsLive, MatchesPrefix, NumberOfNewerVersions. Actions are applied daily automatically.

q: Your application uploads sensitive customer data to Cloud Storage and must comply with regulations requiring encryption key rotation every 90 days. What should you implement?
opts: Use customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) in Cloud KMS with automatic rotation enabled
Use standard Google-managed encryption keys and trigger automatic monthly KMS key rotation API calls using Cloud Functions
Use customer-supplied encryption keys (CSEK) with a key rotation script
Enable object versioning to maintain copies encrypted with different keys
explain: Cloud KMS supports automatic key rotation for CMEK. When enabled, KMS creates a new key version every 90 days (configurable). Objects are encrypted with the active version, but KMS maintains old versions to decrypt existing objects - this enables transparent rotation. Set default_kms_key_name on the bucket. Google-managed keys are rotated automatically but you can't control the schedule. CSEK requires you to manage keys and rotation (complex and error-prone). Object versioning creates copies but doesn't rotate encryption keys.
concept: Encryption key rotation with CMEK: Enable automatic rotation in Cloud KMS (period: 30-90-365 days). New objects use new key version, old objects decrypt with old versions. Transparent to applications. CMEK = Cloud KMS manages keys, auto-rotation supported. CSEK = you manage keys, must handle rotation.

q: You need to grant a service account permission to read all objects in a Cloud Storage bucket but not list the bucket or modify objects. What IAM role should you grant?
opts: Storage Object Viewer (roles/storage.objectViewer) at the bucket level
Storage Object Admin (roles/storage.objectAdmin) at the bucket level
Storage Legacy Bucket Reader (roles/storage.legacyBucketReader) at the bucket level
Storage Object Creator (roles/storage.objectCreator) at the bucket level
explain: Storage Object Viewer grants storage.objects.get and storage.objects.list permissions on objects, allowing reading object content. It does NOT grant bucket-level list permission (storage.buckets.list), meeting the 'not list bucket' requirement. Storage Object Admin grants write/delete permissions (too broad). Legacy Bucket Reader grants bucket list permission (violates requirement). Storage Object Creator is for writes, not reads. Note: The service account can still list objects with storage.objects.list, but can't list buckets or enumerate bucket contents without knowing object names.
concept: Storage IAM roles: Object Viewer = read objects. Object Creator = write new objects. Object Admin = read/write/delete objects. Legacy Bucket Reader = read objects + list bucket. Legacy Object Reader = read objects only (deprecated, use Object Viewer). Grant least privilege.

q: Your data engineering team needs to create BigQuery datasets and tables in project 'prod-analytics' but should not be able to query or view data. What IAM role should you grant?
opts: BigQuery Data Owner (roles/bigquery.dataOwner) at the project level
BigQuery Admin (roles/bigquery.admin) at the project level
BigQuery Data Editor (roles/bigquery.dataEditor) at the project level
BigQuery Job User + Storage Admin at the project level
explain: BigQuery Data Editor grants permissions to create/modify datasets and tables (bigquery.datasets.create, bigquery.tables.create) but does NOT grant query permissions (bigquery.tables.getData, bigquery.jobs.create). Data Owner includes query permissions (too broad). BigQuery Admin grants full BigQuery access including queries and admin operations. Job User + Storage Admin doesn't include dataset/table creation permissions. Data Editor is designed for exactly this: managing schema/structure without data access.
concept: BigQuery IAM separation: Data Owner = full data access. Data Editor = create/modify schema, no query. Data Viewer = query, no modify. Job User = run jobs (required for queries). User = run jobs + own datasets. Admin = all operations. Combine roles for specific needs.

q: Your application uses a service account to access multiple GCP services (BigQuery, Cloud Storage, Pub/Sub). The security team requires least privilege and service-specific credentials. What should you implement?
opts: Create separate service accounts for each service (e.g., bigquery-sa@, storage-sa@) with minimal roles, use in application
Use one service account with separate keys for each service
Grant the service account all necessary roles and use Workload Identity
Use a single service account with roles/viewer and enable API-specific permissions
explain: Principle of least privilege: create service-specific service accounts, each with only the permissions needed for its service. E.g., bigquery-sa@ gets BigQuery Data Viewer only, storage-sa@ gets Storage Object Viewer only. Use different accounts in different parts of your application. Multiple keys for one account doesn't separate permissions. One account with all roles violates least privilege (if account is compromised, all services are exposed). Viewer role is too broad. Workload Identity is for GKE/Cloud Run, not general use.
concept: Service account best practices: Separate service accounts per service/function. Grant minimal roles. Use Workload Identity (GKE/Cloud Run) or service account impersonation. Rotate keys regularly. Avoid user-managed keys when possible (use metadata server, Workload Identity).

q: Your Cloud Function needs to access BigQuery in project A but runs in project B. The security team prohibits sharing service account keys. How should you configure access?
opts: Use the Cloud Function's default service account with Cross-project IAM: grant BigQuery Data Viewer in project A to the function's service account from project B
Export a service account key from project A and store it in Secret Manager in project B
Use service account impersonation: the function's SA impersonates a project A service account
Create a BigQuery authorized view in project B pointing to project A tables
explain: Cloud Functions run with a service account identity (default: PROJECT_ID@appspot.gserviceaccount.com). Grant this service account IAM permissions in project A's BigQuery resources using cross-project IAM. No keys needed - the function authenticates using its runtime identity. Exporting keys violates security policy. Service account impersonation works but adds unnecessary complexity for this simple case. Authorized views solve data access across projects but don't address function authentication.
concept: Cross-project IAM: Grant roles to service accounts from other projects (use full SA email). Common for: shared resources, cross-project pipelines, centralized services. No keys needed - uses Google's authentication. Principle: resources grant access to identities (SAs), regardless of project.

q: Your BigQuery dataset contains PII and must comply with GDPR. You need to grant analysts access to run queries but prevent them from exporting or downloading data. What should you do?
opts: Grant BigQuery Data Viewer role and set authorized views that filter PII fields
Grant BigQuery Job User role only (not Data Viewer), preventing direct table access
Use BigQuery's data exfiltration prevention with trusted zones and VPC-SC perimeter
Grant read-only access via a Service Account with conditional IAM policies
explain: BigQuery Data Viewer grants query access but also export capabilities. VPC Service Controls with data exfiltration prevention is the proper solution: define trusted zones (allowed BigQuery projects/datasets) and create a perimeter that prevents data export outside the perimeter. Queries within the perimeter work, but exports/copies to unauthorized projects fail. Authorized views filter columns but don't prevent exports. Job User alone doesn't grant data access at all (analysts can't query). Conditional IAM policies don't restrict export operations at the granularity needed.
concept: BigQuery data exfiltration prevention: Use VPC Service Controls to create perimeter around BigQuery resources. Define authorized destinations for data access. Prevents copying/exporting data outside perimeter. Combines with column-level security for PII protection.

q: You need to load 500 GB of CSV files from Cloud Storage into BigQuery daily. Files have varying schema updates (new columns added monthly). What loading strategy ensures reliability and handles schema evolution?
opts: Use BigQuery load jobs with schema auto-detection and SCHEMA_UPDATE_OPTIONS allowing field additions
Use bq load with --replace flag to overwrite table with new schema each day
Use BigQuery streaming inserts with schema auto-update
Insert records into an external BigQuery staging table, run schema-conformance validation checks via a PySpark pipeline, and perform a SQL merge to target
explain: BigQuery load jobs support schema auto-detection and SCHEMA_UPDATE_OPTIONS with ALLOW_FIELD_ADDITION, which automatically adds new columns without failing the load. This handles schema evolution gracefully. Load jobs are optimized for batch loading (free, efficient for GB+ data). Using --replace overwrites data, losing history. Streaming inserts are expensive for 500 GB/day and have quotas. Manual staging adds operational overhead and delay. Auto-detection with field addition is the production-recommended pattern for evolving schemas.
concept: BigQuery schema evolution: Use SCHEMA_UPDATE_OPTIONS with ALLOW_FIELD_ADDITION (adds new columns), ALLOW_FIELD_RELAXATION (widens types). Schema auto-detect infers from data. Best for: batch loads with evolving sources. Test schema changes before production with dry-run.

q: Your streaming pipeline inserts data into BigQuery at 10,000 rows/second. You notice high costs and quota errors. What optimization should you apply?
opts: Switch from streaming inserts to BigQuery Storage Write API with batching
Increase the streaming insert quota limit
Reduce row frequency by aggregating before insertion
Use load jobs instead of streaming inserts
explain: BigQuery Storage Write API (introduced 2021) is the modern replacement for legacy streaming inserts. It provides: lower cost (50-75% cheaper), higher throughput, exactly-once semantics, and no quotas. It supports batching for efficiency. This is Google's recommended approach for streaming ingestion. Increasing quotas costs more and doesn't solve the cost issue. Aggregating before insertion reduces rows but loses detail (depends on requirements). Load jobs are for batch (not real-time) and require writing to Cloud Storage first.
concept: BigQuery ingestion methods: Storage Write API = streaming, low-cost, high throughput, exactly-once. Legacy streaming = expensive, 100k rows/sec limit. Load jobs = batch, free, from Cloud Storage. Choose based on latency needs: immediate = Storage Write API, minutes = load jobs.

q: Your pipeline loads JSON files into BigQuery. Recently, loads fail with 'invalid JSON: expected value' errors. The source system occasionally produces malformed JSON. You need to load valid rows and log errors. What should you do?
opts: Use BigQuery load job with max_bad_records setting to allow errors up to threshold
Pre-validate JSON with Cloud Functions before loading
Use Dataflow to parse JSON, filter invalid rows to error table, load valid rows to BigQuery
Switch to CSV format to avoid JSON parsing issues
explain: Dataflow provides full control over error handling: parse JSON with try/catch, route invalid rows to a dead-letter table, load valid rows to BigQuery. This ensures no data loss and provides visibility into errors. BigQuery's max_bad_records allows some errors but doesn't give you the bad records for debugging. Pre-validation with Cloud Functions works but doesn't scale as well as Dataflow for large volumes. Switching to CSV doesn't solve data quality issues and JSON may be required for nested structures.
concept: Error handling pattern: Use Dataflow for complex error handling (dead-letter tables, retries, monitoring). BigQuery native loads for simple cases with max_bad_records. Pattern: parse → route errors to side output → write to error table. Enables debugging and reprocessing.

q: You need to reserve 500 slots for your analytics team's BigQuery queries to ensure consistent performance. The team runs queries 8 hours/day, 5 days/week. What reservation strategy minimizes costs?
opts: Purchase flex slots reservation for baseline (100 slots) and use on-demand for additional capacity during peak usage
Purchase baseline slots (500 slots) with annual commitment
Use on-demand pricing exclusively to avoid over-provisioning
Purchase monthly flex slots (500 slots) for the months when usage is high
explain: Flex slots (monthly or annual commitment) provide reserved capacity at lower cost than on-demand. However, since usage is only 8h/day, 5 days/week (≈24% utilization), reserving full 500 slots 24/7 is wasteful. The optimal strategy is baseline reservation for predictable load (e.g., 100 slots) + on-demand for spikes. You can also use autoscaling with slot reservations. Annual commitment for 500 slots at 24% utilization costs more than mixed approach. Pure on-demand is expensive for consistent usage. Monthly flex slots don't address daily underutilization.
concept: BigQuery slot reservations: On-demand = $6.25/TB, fair scheduling. Flat-rate = reserved slots, predictable cost. Flex slots = monthly/annual commitment, 40-50% discount vs on-demand. Use: baseline reservation + autoscaling or on-demand for peaks. Monitor slot utilization.

q: Your BigQuery queries are processing 500 GB of data daily across multiple tables with similar schemas. Users frequently query the last 30 days of data filtered by customer_region and event_date columns. Query costs are exceeding budget. What is the MOST cost-effective optimization strategy?
opts: Enable BI Engine and increase memory allocation to 100 GB
Partition tables by event_date and cluster by customer_region
Create materialized views for common query patterns
Increase BigQuery slot reservations from 100 to 500 slots
explain: Partitioning by event_date allows BigQuery to prune partitions outside the queried date range, while clustering by customer_region further reduces data scanned within partitions. This directly reduces bytes processed and costs. BI Engine caches results but doesn't reduce initial query costs. Materialized views help with repeated identical queries but add storage costs and maintenance overhead. Increasing slots improves performance but increases costs rather than reducing them.
concept: Cost reduction hierarchy: Partition (prune data) → Cluster (organize within partitions) → Cache/Materialize (reuse results). Partitioning provides the largest cost savings by reducing bytes scanned.

q: A BigQuery query joining three large tables (10 TB, 5 TB, and 100 GB) is timing out after 6 hours. The query execution details show the largest table is being broadcast to all workers. How should you optimize this query?
opts: Add a WHERE clause to filter the 10 TB table before joining
Use APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT instead of COUNT(DISTINCT) for aggregations
Reorder the join to place the smallest table (100 GB) first in the FROM clause
Enable query result caching and retry the query
explain: BigQuery uses the first table in a join as the basis for distribution strategy. Placing the smallest table first allows BigQuery to broadcast the smaller table and shuffle the larger tables more efficiently, reducing data movement. While filtering with WHERE is good practice, it doesn't address the join strategy issue. APPROX functions help with aggregation performance but not join optimization. Result caching doesn't help with queries that are timing out on first execution.
concept: BigQuery join optimization: Smallest table first. The first table in the FROM clause influences distribution strategy. Memory aid: 'Small leads, large follows.'

q: Your data warehouse team reports that BigQuery queries run faster in the morning but slow down significantly in the afternoon when batch ETL jobs run. Your project uses on-demand pricing. What should you investigate FIRST?
opts: Check if afternoon queries are hitting the 2000 concurrent slots limit for on-demand
Review Cloud Logging for BigQuery job quota errors
Analyze query execution plans to identify slot wait times
Switch to flat-rate pricing with baseline 500 slots
explain: Analyzing query execution plans and slot wait times in the execution details provides concrete evidence of slot contention before making infrastructure changes. This diagnostic step identifies whether slowdowns are due to slot availability, query inefficiency, or other factors. While on-demand has a 2000 slot limit per project, checking execution plans confirms this is the actual bottleneck. Reviewing logs is useful but less specific than execution plan analysis. Switching pricing models is premature without understanding the root cause.
concept: Performance troubleshooting methodology: Measure before migrating. Use query execution details to diagnose slot contention (wait times) versus query inefficiency (stage durations).

q: A partitioned BigQuery table contains 3 years of daily data. Queries filtering on the partition column (ingestion_date) are still scanning more data than expected. The _PARTITIONTIME pseudo-column is used in WHERE clauses. What is the likely cause?
opts: The table uses ingestion-time partitioning but queries need column-based partitioning
Partition pruning is disabled due to non-constant expressions in the WHERE clause
The table requires clustering in addition to partitioning
BigQuery automatically scans adjacent partitions for query optimization
explain: When using _PARTITIONTIME with an ingestion_date filter, there's a mismatch: _PARTITIONTIME refers to when data was loaded, not the ingestion_date column value. Column-based partitioning on ingestion_date would allow proper partition pruning. Non-constant expressions can prevent pruning, but the question indicates filters are on the partition column. Clustering is complementary but doesn't explain excessive scanning. BigQuery doesn't automatically scan adjacent partitions unless the query logic requires it.
concept: Partition pruning requirements: Match partition column to query filter. Ingestion-time (_PARTITIONTIME) vs column-based partitioning must align with query patterns. Use column-based for business date filtering.

q: Your BigQuery queries against a 2 TB table consistently show 'Slot wait time' exceeding 3 minutes during peak hours. You have 100 baseline flat-rate slots. Which action provides the MOST immediate improvement?
opts: Purchase 400 additional flex slots for peak hours only
Enable result caching and schedule queries during off-peak hours
Partition and cluster the table to reduce bytes processed
Use reservations to prioritize critical workloads over batch jobs
explain: Flex slots provide immediate additional capacity that can be purchased hourly for peak periods, directly addressing the slot shortage indicated by wait times. While table optimization (partitioning/clustering) reduces bytes processed, it doesn't increase available slots for concurrent workloads. Result caching helps repeated queries but not initial executions. Reservations reorganize existing slot allocation but don't add capacity. The question specifically mentions slot wait time, indicating insufficient compute capacity.
concept: Slot wait time → capacity problem. Solution hierarchy for slot contention: Add flex slots (immediate) → Optimize queries (reduce demand) → Use reservations (prioritize workloads).

q: A Dataproc cluster processes daily batch jobs that run for 2 hours. The cluster has 10 worker nodes (n1-standard-4) and remains idle for 22 hours daily. Job completion time is acceptable. What is the MOST cost-effective optimization?
opts: Use preemptible VMs for all worker nodes and enable graceful decommissioning
Enable autoscaling with minimum 2 workers and maximum 10 workers
Use Cloud Scheduler to create ephemeral clusters daily and delete after job completion
Reduce worker nodes to 5 and increase machine type to n1-standard-8
explain: Ephemeral clusters eliminate idle time costs entirely by creating clusters only when needed and deleting them immediately after completion, saving 22 hours of compute costs daily. Preemptible VMs save 80% on compute but still incur costs during idle periods. Autoscaling reduces idle nodes but master node and minimum workers still run continuously. Changing machine types doesn't address the fundamental issue of paying for idle resources. For batch workloads with predictable schedules, ephemeral clusters are most cost-effective.
concept: Dataproc cost optimization: Ephemeral clusters for batch, persistent for interactive. Decision rule: If idle time > job runtime, use ephemeral clusters with workflow templates.

q: A Dataproc cluster running Spark jobs experiences frequent worker node preemptions (30% of nodes terminated within 4 hours). Jobs fail with 'Stage failed due to fetch failures.' What is the BEST solution to maintain cost savings while improving reliability?
opts: Increase the percentage of non-preemptible worker nodes to 50%
Enable graceful decommissioning with a 30-minute timeout
Use preemptible VMs only for secondary workers, not primary workers
Configure Spark to write shuffle data to Cloud Storage instead of local disk
explain: Using preemptible VMs only for secondary workers (separate from primary workers) allows Spark to lose secondary workers without losing shuffle data, as primary workers retain the data. Secondary workers can be 100% preemptible for maximum savings. Graceful decommissioning helps but doesn't prevent fetch failures if preemption rate is high. Increasing non-preemptible ratio improves reliability but reduces cost savings. Writing shuffle to GCS significantly degrades performance and increases costs due to network I/O.
concept: Dataproc preemptible VM strategy: Primary workers (non-preemptible) hold shuffle data, secondary workers (preemptible) provide extra capacity. Configure YARN/HDFS on primaries only.

q: A Dataproc cluster processes variable workloads throughout the day. Autoscaling is enabled with scale-up factor 1.0 and cooldown period 2 minutes. Jobs still experience delays during sudden traffic spikes. What should you adjust?
opts: Decrease cooldown period to 30 seconds for faster scaling response
Increase scale-up factor to 2.0 to add more workers per scaling event
Decrease scale-down cooldown to reclaim idle capacity faster
Enable graceful decommissioning to allow safer scale-down operations
explain: Increasing the scale-up factor adds more workers per scaling decision, helping the cluster respond more aggressively to sudden demand spikes. The 2-minute cooldown is already reasonable; decreasing it below 2 minutes can cause thrashing. Scale-down cooldown and graceful decommissioning improve cost efficiency and stability during scale-down but don't help with scale-up responsiveness during traffic spikes. The question specifically identifies delays during spikes, indicating insufficient scale-up aggressiveness.
concept: Dataproc autoscaling tuning: Scale-up factor controls aggressiveness (how many workers), cooldown controls frequency (how often). For bursty workloads: increase factor, keep cooldown ≥ 2min.

q: A Cloud Composer DAG has 15 tasks with complex dependencies. Five tasks are external API calls that occasionally fail due to rate limiting. The DAG uses default retry settings (1 retry, no delay). What is the BEST configuration to improve resilience?
opts: Set retries=3 and retry_delay=300s (5 minutes) on API call tasks only
Set retries=5 and retry_exponential_backoff=True for the entire DAG
Implement a custom sensor to check API availability before executing tasks
Use Airflow's SimpleHttpOperator with connection pooling enabled
explain: Configuring task-specific retries with appropriate delay addresses rate limiting directly on the problematic tasks without affecting other tasks. Setting 5-minute delays allows rate limits to reset between retries. Applying retries to the entire DAG is inefficient for tasks that don't need them. Custom sensors add complexity and don't handle mid-task failures. SimpleHttpOperator doesn't solve rate limiting issues; the retry strategy does. Exponential backoff is useful but fixed 5-minute delay is more predictable for known rate limit windows.
concept: Airflow retry strategy: Task-specific over DAG-level. For rate limiting: retries=3-5 with retry_delay matching rate limit window. Memory aid: 'Target the task, time the retry.'

q: A Cloud Composer environment frequently experiences task delays during 9-11 AM when 50 DAGs schedule concurrently. Airflow webserver shows scheduler heartbeat gaps. Current configuration: 1 scheduler, 4 workers (n1-standard-4), 20 max_active_runs per DAG. What should you optimize FIRST?
opts: Increase worker count to 8 to handle more concurrent tasks
Add a second scheduler to improve DAG parsing and task scheduling throughput
Reduce max_active_runs to 10 to decrease scheduler load
Upgrade worker machine type to n1-standard-8 for more CPU per worker
explain: Scheduler heartbeat gaps indicate scheduler bottleneck during DAG parsing and scheduling, not task execution capacity. Adding a second scheduler (available in Airflow 2.x) improves scheduling throughput for the 50 concurrent DAGs. Worker count affects task execution capacity, but the issue is scheduling delays before tasks even start. Reducing max_active_runs limits parallelism and doesn't solve the scheduler bottleneck. Upgrading worker machine type doesn't help scheduling performance.
concept: Composer bottleneck diagnosis: Scheduler heartbeat gaps = add schedulers. Worker queue buildup = add workers. Decision: Scheduling delays → more schedulers; Execution delays → more workers.

q: A DAG uses a FileSensor to wait for files in Cloud Storage before processing. Files arrive unpredictably between 1-6 AM. The sensor checks every 60 seconds with timeout=10800s (3 hours). The environment frequently hits Composer worker limits during sensor wait times. How should you optimize this pattern?
opts: Increase poke_interval to 300 seconds (5 minutes) to reduce worker slot consumption
Switch sensor mode from 'poke' to 'reschedule' to free worker slots while waiting
Use GCSObjectUpdateSensor instead of FileSensor for better Cloud Storage integration
Replace the sensor with a Cloud Storage trigger that starts the DAG when files arrive
explain: Cloud Storage triggers (Pub/Sub notifications) eliminate active waiting entirely, starting the DAG only when files actually arrive. This frees all worker slots and scales perfectly regardless of wait time. Reschedule mode helps but sensors still consume scheduler resources and add complexity. Increasing poke_interval reduces frequency but doesn't eliminate the problem. GCSObjectUpdateSensor is functionally similar to FileSensor and doesn't solve the worker consumption issue. Event-driven architecture is superior to polling for unpredictable arrivals.
concept: Airflow sensor anti-pattern: Polling wastes resources. For external events (GCS files, Pub/Sub messages): Use triggers/Pub/Sub, not sensors. Sensors are for systems without event APIs.

q: Your data pipeline validates incoming data quality before loading to BigQuery. You need to check that 'revenue' column values are positive, 'customer_id' has no nulls, and 'order_date' is within the last 90 days. Which approach provides the BEST balance of expressiveness and maintainability?
opts: Write custom Python validation functions using pandas and assert statements
Use Great Expectations with built-in expectations (expect_column_values_to_be_between, expect_column_values_to_not_be_null)
Create BigQuery SQL queries with WHERE clauses to filter invalid records
Use Dataprep recipes with data quality rules and thresholds
explain: Great Expectations provides declarative, reusable expectations with built-in validation logic, automatic documentation, and data quality profiling. It's designed specifically for this use case and integrates well with data pipelines. Custom pandas code requires more maintenance and lacks standardization. BigQuery SQL can validate but doesn't provide comprehensive reporting, versioning, or expectation catalogs. Dataprep is interactive and not ideal for automated pipeline validation. Great Expectations offers the best combination of expressiveness, testing, and governance.
concept: Data quality framework selection: Great Expectations for structured validation at scale. Decision rule: Use GE when rules are reusable, need documentation, or require expectation suites across datasets.

q: A data pipeline processes 100K records daily. You've implemented Great Expectations validation that checks 20 expectations per batch. Validation time increased from 2 minutes to 15 minutes as dataset size grew. What optimization should you apply?
opts: Sample 10% of records for validation instead of validating the entire dataset
Reduce the number of expectations from 20 to 5 critical checks only
Enable parallel execution of expectations using execution_engine='spark'
Move validation to BigQuery using SQL-based expectations instead of pandas
explain: Using Spark as the execution engine allows Great Expectations to parallelize expectation evaluation across distributed compute, significantly improving performance for large datasets. Sampling introduces risk of missing data quality issues in unsampled records. Reducing expectations compromises data quality coverage. Moving to BigQuery SQL might help but loses Great Expectations' rich features and standardization. The pandas execution engine processes serially, which explains the performance degradation with data growth.
concept: Great Expectations performance: Scale execution engine with data volume. Decision: Pandas (<1M rows) → Spark (1M-100M rows) → SQL/BigQuery (>100M rows). Keep expectations comprehensive, scale compute.

q: You need to validate that 'email' column values match standard email format and that 'country_code' values are in a predefined list of 50 countries. Great Expectations doesn't have a built-in email format expectation. What is the BEST approach?
opts: Use expect_column_values_to_match_regex for email and expect_column_values_to_be_in_set for country codes
Create a custom expectation class extending ExpectationConfiguration for email validation
Use expect_column_values_to_match_strftime_format for email validation
Implement validation in Python before Great Expectations and only use GE for simple checks
explain: expect_column_values_to_match_regex is perfect for email format validation using standard regex patterns, while expect_column_values_to_be_in_set directly supports the country code validation use case. Both are built-in expectations that require no custom code. Creating custom expectations is unnecessary overhead when built-in expectations suffice. strftime_format is for datetime parsing, not email validation. Splitting validation between systems adds complexity and defeats the purpose of using a validation framework.
concept: Great Expectations coverage: Use built-in expectations first (match_regex, be_in_set, be_between). Custom expectations only when logic is truly unique. Regex handles most pattern validation needs.

q: Your BigQuery streaming pipeline ingests 10K events/second. You need to alert when event processing latency exceeds 5 minutes. Cloud Monitoring shows BigQuery metrics but no latency metric exists. What is the MOST effective monitoring approach?
opts: Create a logs-based metric from BigQuery audit logs measuring timestamp differences
Write a custom Cloud Function to query BigQuery every minute and calculate latency
Add application-level timestamps to events and create BigQuery scheduled queries to calculate latency
Use Cloud Monitoring's Metrics Explorer to create a derived metric from existing BigQuery metrics
explain: Adding application timestamps (event_time) to the data allows you to calculate processing latency (current_time - event_time) directly in BigQuery scheduled queries, which can then export results to Cloud Monitoring for alerting. This approach is reliable and doesn't depend on log parsing. Logs-based metrics from audit logs may not capture streaming insert timestamps reliably. Cloud Functions add unnecessary complexity and execution costs. Metrics Explorer can't derive latency from standard BigQuery metrics that don't include timing information.
concept: Custom metric strategy: Instrument at source, calculate in pipeline. For latency: Add timestamp at ingestion, compute delta in processing layer, export to monitoring. Pattern: Source → Pipeline → Monitor.

q: A data pipeline has an SLO of 99.5% availability (monthly window). You need to create an alerting policy that notifies the team when SLO burn rate indicates you'll exhaust the error budget in 2 days. What should you configure?
opts: Alert when error rate exceeds 0.5% in any 5-minute window
Configure a burn rate threshold of 14.4x (consumption in 2 days) over a 1-hour window
Set up a simple alert when availability drops below 99.5% for 10 minutes
Query Cloud Billing exports and GCS access logs within a custom visual notebook to audit error-budget spend on a weekly schedule
explain: Burn rate alerting calculates how fast you're consuming error budget. For a 30-day monthly budget exhausted in 2 days, the burn rate is 15x (30/2). The option suggests 14.4x which is close (assuming ~28.8 day month). Alerting on burn rate over a window (1 hour) provides early warning before SLO violation. Simple error rate threshold doesn't account for the budget concept. Availability threshold alerts after SLO is already violated. Manual monitoring doesn't scale and lacks automation. Google SRE recommends multi-window, multi-burn-rate alerts.
concept: SLO alerting: Use burn rate, not absolute thresholds. Formula: burn_rate = time_window / target_exhaustion_time. Fast burn (2 days) needs shorter detection window (1 hour). Google SRE pattern: 2% in 1hr (36x burn).

q: Cloud Monitoring shows high memory usage on Dataproc workers, but default GCE memory metrics don't provide JVM heap visibility needed for Spark tuning. What should you configure to get JVM-level metrics?
opts: Enable Cloud Monitoring agent on Dataproc nodes to collect custom JVM metrics
Use Dataproc's built-in initialization actions to install Prometheus exporters for JVM metrics
Query Spark UI REST API periodically and export metrics to Cloud Monitoring using custom Cloud Function
Enable Dataproc Component Gateway and access Spark History Server for JVM metrics
explain: Dataproc initialization actions can install and configure Prometheus JMX exporters that expose JVM metrics, which can then be scraped and sent to Cloud Monitoring. This is the standard pattern for custom metrics on Dataproc clusters. The Cloud Monitoring agent provides OS-level metrics but not JVM-specific metrics. Cloud Functions polling Spark UI adds unnecessary complexity and latency. Component Gateway provides UI access but doesn't export metrics to monitoring systems for alerting and analysis.
concept: Dataproc observability layers: GCE metrics (OS/hardware) → Initialization actions + Prometheus (JVM/application) → Cloud Monitoring (aggregation/alerts). Custom metrics need exporters installed at cluster creation.

q: Your data warehouse team needs to distinguish between user queries (interactive) and ETL jobs (batch) in Cloud Monitoring dashboards for BigQuery. Both use the same project but different service accounts. What is the SIMPLEST approach?
opts: Add labels to BigQuery jobs using the 'labels' parameter in job configurations
Create separate BigQuery reservations for interactive and batch workloads
Use Cloud Monitoring to filter metrics by principal_email (service account) in dashboard queries
Write a Cloud Function to tag jobs after submission based on user identity
explain: Filtering Cloud Monitoring metrics by principal_email leverages existing authentication information without requiring code changes or infrastructure modifications. The principal_email dimension is automatically captured in BigQuery audit logs and metrics. Job labels require modifying all job submission code. Separate reservations provide resource isolation but are overkill if you only need monitoring separation. Cloud Functions add complexity and lag. The simplest solution uses existing metadata for filtering.
concept: Monitoring segmentation strategy: Use existing dimensions first (user, job type, service account), add labels only when needed. Decision tree: Existing dimension? Use it. Need custom grouping? Add labels.

q: A data pipeline extracts data from Cloud SQL hourly, transforms it using Dataflow, and loads into BigQuery. The source table has an updated_at timestamp. How should you implement incremental processing to minimize data transfer and processing?
opts: Track the last processed updated_at timestamp in Cloud Storage and filter source query using WHERE updated_at > last_processed
Use BigQuery's _TABLE_SUFFIX to partition data by hour and only process new partitions
Implement Change Data Capture (CDC) using Datastream to capture only changed records
Read all data and use Dataflow's Combine transform to deduplicate records based on updated_at
explain: Storing the last processed timestamp (watermark) and filtering the source query is the standard ELT pattern for incremental loads from databases with update timestamps. This minimizes data extracted and processed. CDC via Datastream is powerful but adds infrastructure complexity when simple timestamp filtering suffices. _TABLE_SUFFIX is for BigQuery wildcard queries, not source extraction. Reading all data defeats the purpose of incremental processing. The timestamp watermark pattern is simple, efficient, and idiomatic.
concept: Incremental ELT pattern: Store watermark (last timestamp) → Filter source with WHERE timestamp > watermark → Update watermark after success. Storage: Cloud Storage, Datastore, or metadata table.

q: A batch ETL pipeline occasionally fails mid-processing, leaving partial data in BigQuery target tables. Rerunning the job creates duplicate records. The pipeline uses batch INSERT statements. What pattern ensures idempotent loads?
opts: Use BigQuery MERGE statements with unique keys to upsert records instead of INSERT
Truncate target tables before each pipeline run using DELETE statements
Add a job_run_id column to records and filter for max(job_run_id) in queries
Enable BigQuery streaming inserts with deduplication using insertId
explain: MERGE (upsert) statements check for existing records by key and update if present or insert if absent, making the operation idempotent regardless of how many times it runs. Truncating tables loses data if multiple jobs run concurrently and doesn't handle partial failures. Adding job_run_id requires changing query patterns and doesn't prevent duplicates. Streaming inserts with insertId provide deduplication but are designed for streaming workloads, not batch ETL. MERGE is the standard idempotent batch load pattern.
concept: Idempotent load patterns: Batch → MERGE/UPSERT with keys. Streaming → insertId deduplication. DELETE/INSERT is NOT idempotent. Memory aid: 'MERGE makes multiple runs safe.'

q: A BigQuery table has 1,000 columns. Analysts need all columns except 10 sensitive fields, and cost must be minimized. Which query pattern is best?
opts: SELECT * EXCEPT (sensitive_col1, sensitive_col2, ...) FROM `project.dataset.table`
SELECT * FROM `project.dataset.table` and drop columns in BI tool
CREATE TABLE copy AS SELECT * FROM source, then manually remove fields daily
SELECT TO_JSON_STRING(t) FROM `project.dataset.table` t to scan fewer bytes
explain: SELECT * EXCEPT avoids scanning unnecessary columns and is the cost-efficient SQL pattern for excluding a known set of fields.
concept: BigQuery cost optimization: prune columns with SELECT * EXCEPT.

q: You are implementing Authorized Views for analysts to see only masked records from a restricted table. Where should the authorized view live?
opts: In a separate dataset from the source tables, then grant analysts access to that view dataset
In the same dataset as source tables with Data Viewer on the dataset
In the same dataset with BigQuery Admin on the view only
In any dataset because Authorized Views ignore dataset-level permissions
explain: Authorized Views should be placed in a separate dataset so analysts can query the view without inheriting direct visibility to raw source tables through same-dataset access patterns.
concept: BigQuery authorized views security boundary: separate dataset for controlled exposure.

q: Which IAM role allows reading/writing table data but does not grant full dataset administration?
opts: BigQuery Data Editor
BigQuery Admin
BigQuery Data Viewer
Project Owner
explain: BigQuery Data Editor can create/update/delete table data objects in scope, but BigQuery Admin has broader administrative authority. Data Viewer is read-only.
concept: BigQuery IAM role boundaries: Data Viewer vs Data Editor vs Admin.

q: You need to load CSV from Cloud Storage into BigQuery with a defined schema using the CLI. Which command shape is correct?
opts: bq load --source_format=CSV dataset.table gs://bucket/path/file.csv schema.json
bq query --load_csv dataset.table gs://bucket/path/file.csv schema.json
bq extract --source_format=CSV dataset.table gs://bucket/path/file.csv schema.json
bq cp --source_format=CSV gs://bucket/path/file.csv dataset.table schema.json
explain: The correct load command uses bq load with --source_format=CSV followed by destination table, source URI, and schema.
concept: BigQuery CLI: bq load syntax for CSV ingestion.

q: A team grants BigQuery Data Viewer to an analyst group but wants to prevent any accidental table modifications. Is this role appropriate?
opts: Yes, Data Viewer is read-only and cannot modify table data or schema
No, Data Viewer can update table rows but not schema
No, Data Viewer can delete datasets but not tables
No, Data Viewer is equivalent to BigQuery Admin at dataset scope
explain: BigQuery Data Viewer provides read/list access only. It is suitable when modifications must be blocked.
concept: BigQuery least privilege: Data Viewer for strict read-only access.
